html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty-Two -- Complete

Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice

4QShirShabb

Thirteen Sabbath Angelic Liturgies -- DSS Attested

Five Representative Songs Preserved

Praise the Creator of Divine Knowledge All You Holy Angels -- The Seven Angelic Priesthoods -- The Seventh Sabbath on the Forty-Ninth Day -- The Chariot Throne -- The Sound of Their Blessing Is Like the Roar of Many Waters

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 4 Qumran -- 4Q400-407 -- Hebrew Primary

Confirmed Also at Masada -- Wide Circulation Beyond Qumran

CONCERN LEVEL: MINIMAL -- External reference only -- Zero Christian content

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE -- 4QShirShabb -- SELECTED SONGS

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE — SONG 1 For the First Sabbath on the Fourth of the First Month — Praise the Creator of the Elohim of Knowledge — All the Angels of the Holy of Holies — Praise the Most High Creator

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE and confirmed at Masada — predating all New Testament documents. The angelic liturgy described in these songs is rooted entirely in Yehezkel's throne-chariot vision and the ancient Afro-Asiatic covenant priestly tradition. The divine beings, angelic priests, and chariot throne described here belong to the authentic pre-Christian covenant cosmological framework. The minimal concern noted is entirely external: certain early post-Christian mystical writers who were aware of Jewish liturgical traditions later referenced similar angelic worship themes. This borrowing occurred after the fact and had nothing to do with the original text or the Qumran covenant community's theology. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written independently of and before any Christian tradition. Read this text as what it is: authentic Qumran covenant liturgy celebrating the heavenly worship of Creator — with zero connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the Maskil. Song of the burnt offering of the first Sabbath on the fourth of the first month. Praise the Creator of divine knowledge, all you holy angels.

2 He is holy and sanctifies himself with the knowledge of all the holy ones.

3 All those who are mighty in his word shall not rest until they accomplish all that he has decreed.

4 For Creator made us and he is the Creator of all divine beings and the Prince of Light from the first of his holy creations.

5 The Most High Creator, the Creator of the heavens and the earth — how great is his glory above all the holy ones.

6 And how wonderful are his mighty works among the divine beings, and how great his goodness among all the holy ones.

7 His spirit guides them in all the ways of his truth and his word leads them in the paths of his holiness.

8 Praise his name and all you divine beings praise his glory — exalt his name above all the holy ones.

9 He has made among the divine beings seven priesthoods of his holiness, and seven councils of his glory.

10 And he has established for himself seven great princes among all the holy ones — princes of pure and holy wonder.

11 Praise the mighty Creator with his holy spirit and exalt him for ever and ever in his glory.

12 Praise the Creator of knowledge — all you angels of his holiness — and all you who are called by his name, sanctify him together.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice (4QShirShabb) was found in multiple copies at Qumran Cave 4 — designated 4Q400-407 — and one copy at Masada (MasShirShabb), confirming its wide circulation beyond the Qumran sectarian community. The document consists of thirteen Sabbath songs, one for each of the first thirteen Sabbaths of the year, structured according to the 364-day solar calendar. Song 1, for the first Sabbath of the first month, establishes the foundational theology of the entire cycle: the angelic worship in the heavenly sanctuary that mirrors and grounds the earthly community's Sabbath worship.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Yehezkel's throne-chariot vision in Chapters 1 and 10 in full — the Merkabah tradition that provides the foundational imagery for the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice. The heavenly throne, the four living creatures, the wheels within wheels, and the glory of Creator above them all are the theological background of the angelic liturgy described in the Songs. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves the angelic worship tradition of Chanokh Chapters 14-16 and 71, which provides direct precedent for the Songs' description of angelic liturgical service.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yehezkel 1 and Tehillim 29 and 99 provide the covenant foundational texts for Song 1's angelic praise. The seven priesthoods of holiness and seven councils of glory reflect the Qumran community's theological development of the priestly service structure into the heavenly realm: the earthly priestly service of the sons of Tzadok has its heavenly counterpart in the angelic priestly service of the seven holy princes. The earthly sabbath worship participates in the heavenly sabbath worship.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Song 1's structure — establishing seven angelic priesthoods and seven councils — reflects the Qumran community's characteristic numerical theology: seven is the number of covenant completeness. The seven songs of each Sabbath cycle complete the thirteen-Sabbath quarter of the year. The seven angelic priesthoods complete the heavenly priestly structure. The entire cosmological framework of the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice is built on the number seven as the covenant signature of Creator's ordered reality.

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE — SONG 4 For the Fourth Sabbath — The Divine Beings Bless the Wonders of the Creator — The Chariot Throne of His Glory — The Seven Angelic Princes — How Wonderful Are the Wonders

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE and confirmed at Masada — predating all New Testament documents. The angelic liturgy described in these songs is rooted entirely in Yehezkel's throne-chariot vision and the ancient Afro-Asiatic covenant priestly tradition. The divine beings, angelic priests, and chariot throne described here belong to the authentic pre-Christian covenant cosmological framework. The minimal concern noted is entirely external: certain early post-Christian mystical writers who were aware of Jewish liturgical traditions later referenced similar angelic worship themes. This borrowing occurred after the fact and had nothing to do with the original text or the Qumran covenant community's theology. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written independently of and before any Christian tradition. Read this text as what it is: authentic Qumran covenant liturgy celebrating the heavenly worship of Creator — with zero connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the Maskil. Song of the burnt offering of the fourth Sabbath on the twenty-fifth of the first month.

2 Praise the Creator of the high ones, all you angels of holiness — bless the King of glory with his holy spirit.

3 The divine beings praise his wonderful works and all the holy ones sing the splendor of his holiness.

4 He has blessed the first of his priests above all who minister before him forever.

5 And he has appointed the princes of the inner sanctuary of the holy of holies among his wondrous works.

6 The seven priests of the inner sanctuary serve before him in the holy of holies — the most holy ones among the holy ones.

7 The first among them speaks with a mighty voice concerning the wonders of the King of kings in the courts of his glory.

8 And all the divine beings of the holy of holies come before him and sing of his wondrous works.

9 How wonderful are the works of your hands O Creator — how wonderful is the chariot throne of your glory.

10 And the seven deputy priests before him bless and praise the wonderful Creator in his holy dwelling.

11 His holy spirit rests upon all the holy ones and the strength of his glory is among all the princes of light.

12 Sing praises all you who enter into the inner sanctuary of his glory — exalt him forever and ever and bless his holy name always.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q400-407 preserves Song 4 in Hebrew. The fourth Sabbath song introduces the seven angelic priests of the inner sanctuary — the most holy ones — who serve in the heavenly holy of holies. The structure of the heavenly sanctuary mirrors the earthly tabernacle and temple: there is a holy of holies, there are priests who enter it, and there is a liturgy of praise and blessing. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice develops this parallelism systematically across thirteen songs until the full description of the divine chariot throne is reached in Song 13.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Yehezkel 10 — the second chariot vision — in which the cherubim, the wheels, and the glory of Creator are described in their second appearance. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice draws on Yehezkel's repeated throne visions as the authoritative description of the heavenly sanctuary. The divine chariot throne of verse 9 is the Merkabah — the chariot of Creator — whose description is the culminating vision of the entire thirteen-song cycle.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yehezkel 1:22-28 and 10:1-22 provide the foundational covenant images for Song 4's angelic priests and chariot throne. The seven priests of the inner sanctuary correspond to the seven-branched menorah of the tabernacle — the symbol of the perfect light of Creator's covenant presence. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice transforms the material sanctuary objects of the Mosaic covenant into their heavenly archetypes.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The seven deputy priests of verse 10 parallel the seven chief priests of verse 6 — creating a two-tier angelic priestly structure that mirrors the two-tier earthly priestly structure of the Qumran community: the sons of Tzadok over the Levites. The theological import is clear: the Qumran community's priestly hierarchy is not a human invention but a reflection of the heavenly reality. The earthly community's priestly order is grounded in the order of the heavenly sanctuary.

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE — SONG 7 The Seventh Sabbath — The Praise of the Seven Angelic Princes — All the Spirits of Knowledge Bless Him — The Wondrous Praises of the Creator — The Holiness of the King of Holiness

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE and confirmed at Masada — predating all New Testament documents. The angelic liturgy described in these songs is rooted entirely in Yehezkel's throne-chariot vision and the ancient Afro-Asiatic covenant priestly tradition. The divine beings, angelic priests, and chariot throne described here belong to the authentic pre-Christian covenant cosmological framework. The minimal concern noted is entirely external: certain early post-Christian mystical writers who were aware of Jewish liturgical traditions later referenced similar angelic worship themes. This borrowing occurred after the fact and had nothing to do with the original text or the Qumran covenant community's theology. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written independently of and before any Christian tradition. Read this text as what it is: authentic Qumran covenant liturgy celebrating the heavenly worship of Creator — with zero connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the Maskil. Song of the burnt offering of the seventh Sabbath on the forty-ninth day of the year.

2 Praise the Creator of divine knowledge, all you who are exalted among all the holy ones — praise him with wonders in his wonderful holiness.

3 Glorify him O holy ones of the divine beings — glorify him in his royal majesty.

4 The first of his princes shall praise him with seven wondrous words and bless all his holy ones with seven wonderful words.

5 The second of his princes shall bless with seven words of his marvels all those called to righteousness.

6 The third shall tell of the glory of the King of holiness with seven mighty words.

7 The fourth shall speak in wonders seven times with seven words of his wonders and bless all the foundations of the holy of holies.

8 The fifth shall bless with seven wondrous words all those who know the eternal mysteries.

9 The sixth shall give thanks with seven words of his great wonders to the Creator of the holy ones.

10 The seventh of his princes shall bless with seven words all the chosen ones of those formed of knowledge.

11 Then all the holy ones shall bless the Creator of the holy ones with seven words of wondrous holiness.

12 Sing praise seven times to the Creator of holiness, all you divine beings of the holy of holies — blessed be his name forever and ever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Song 7 is the structural centerpiece of the entire thirteen-song cycle — the seventh of thirteen, it marks the midpoint. Its structure is the most symmetrical of all the songs: seven princes each blessing with seven words, followed by all the holy ones blessing with seven words. The number seven multiplied throughout the song is the calendar theology of the Songs in its purest form: the forty-ninth day of the year — seven times seven — is the threshold of the feast of weeks, and the song celebrates the convergence of seven and seven in the liturgical calendar.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves the feast of weeks — Shavuot — as the covenant anniversary of the Torah given at Sinai. The forty-ninth day — seven times seven — is the covenant foundation of the feast that Yovelim Chapter 6 establishes as the covenant renewal feast. Song 7's placement on the forty-ninth day of the year places the heavenly angelic worship at the precise covenant moment that the Orit Ge'ez identifies as the renewal of the covenant between Creator and his people. The angelic praise of Song 7 accompanies the covenant renewal.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 23:15-16 — you shall count seven full weeks from the day after the Sabbath, the day that you brought the sheaf of wave offering — establishes the forty-nine day count that places Song 7 at the feast of weeks. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice maps the thirteen songs onto the first quarter of the covenant year, with Song 7 falling precisely at the covenant feast of covenant renewal. The calendar theology of the songs is inseparable from the covenant theology of the festivals.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The seven times seven structure of Song 7 — seven princes each blessing seven times — creates 49 blessings, which is mathematically the feast of weeks itself embedded in the liturgy. This is the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice at its most theologically refined: the liturgy does not merely accompany the covenant feast — it enacts the covenant feast's mathematical and theological content within the heavenly worship itself. The earthly community celebrating the feast of weeks participates in a heavenly liturgy that is itself structured as the feast of weeks.

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE — SONG 12 The Twelfth Sabbath — The Chariot Throne — The Wheels of the Great Chariot — The Cherubim and the Ophanim — The Wondrous Works of the Living Creatures — The Sound of Their Blessing Is Like the Sound of Many Waters

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE and confirmed at Masada — predating all New Testament documents. The angelic liturgy described in these songs is rooted entirely in Yehezkel's throne-chariot vision and the ancient Afro-Asiatic covenant priestly tradition. The divine beings, angelic priests, and chariot throne described here belong to the authentic pre-Christian covenant cosmological framework. The minimal concern noted is entirely external: certain early post-Christian mystical writers who were aware of Jewish liturgical traditions later referenced similar angelic worship themes. This borrowing occurred after the fact and had nothing to do with the original text or the Qumran covenant community's theology. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written independently of and before any Christian tradition. Read this text as what it is: authentic Qumran covenant liturgy celebrating the heavenly worship of Creator — with zero connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the Maskil. Song of the burnt offering of the twelfth Sabbath on the eighty-fourth day.

2 Praise the Creator of the Most High — all you angelic hosts — bless his glorious name with divine speech.

3 Praise the Creator of divine knowledge in the wonderful dwelling of his glory — for the sound of divine speech is like the sound of many waters.

4 The cherubim prostrate themselves before him and bless — as they arise the sound of divine stillness is heard.

5 And when they raise their wings there is the sound of divine stillness — all the chariots of the inner chambers sing praises together.

6 The wheels of the great chariot are raised up — the holy of holies shines with the glory of the living creatures.

7 And from between the wheels of glory come sounds like fire — the living creatures go and return like the appearance of lightning.

8 The spirit of the living creatures is in the wheels and the sound of their movement is like the sound of a host.

9 And the cherubim bless the chariot throne above the firmament and they praise the splendor of the luminous firmament beneath the throne of his glory.

10 When the living beings move the holy chariot wheels move with them and the holy angels return beside them.

11 From between the wheels come voices like the voice of Creator — the voice of the holy spirit — and the sound of their blessing is like the sound of many waters.

12 Blessed be the Creator of all divine beings from his holy dwelling place forever and ever — blessed is his glorious name above all the holy ones.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q405 and MasShirShabb preserve Song 12 in Hebrew. This is the penultimate song of the thirteen-song cycle and the first of the two great chariot-throne songs that form the climax of the entire collection. Song 12 describes the living creatures, the wheels, and the sounds of the heavenly chariot in the language of Yehezkel 1 and 10, creating a liturgical re-enactment of the Merkabah vision. The sound of divine speech like the sound of many waters — Yehezkel 1:24 and 43:2 — is the characteristic sonic signature of the divine chariot in both Yehezkel and the Songs.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Yehezkel 1 and 10 in full — the primary source texts for Song 12's chariot imagery. The living creatures, the wheels within wheels, the firmament above them, and the throne above the firmament — Yehezkel 1:22-26 — are the architectural elements of the heavenly throne that Song 12 sets to liturgical music. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice does not merely describe the Merkabah — it provides the words that the angelic beings themselves sing as they move and return in the chariot vision.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yehezkel 1:24 — and when they moved I heard the sound of their wings like the sound of many waters, like the sound of the Almighty, the sound of tumult — is the direct source for verse 3 and 11 of Song 12. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice takes the Yehezkel Merkabah vision and transforms it from prophetic description into liturgical participation: the community that sings Song 12 on the twelfth Sabbath is joining its voice with the living creatures and the wheels that surround the throne of Creator's glory.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Song 12's description of the chariot throne is the fullest in the Qumran corpus and one of the most detailed in all ancient covenant literature. The systematic elaboration of Yehezkel's vision into a participatory liturgy reflects the Qumran community's conviction that their Sabbath worship was not separated from the heavenly worship by time or distance — the same praise that the living creatures offer before the throne is offered by the community in their earthly assembly. This participatory heavenly-worship theology is the most distinctive feature of the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice.

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE — SONG 13 The Thirteenth Sabbath — The Full Vision of the Chariot Throne — The Appearance of the Living Creatures — The Robe of the Inner Chambers — The Sound of Their Blessing as the Roar of Many Waters — Glory Above the Chariot Throne

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE and confirmed at Masada — predating all New Testament documents. The angelic liturgy described in these songs is rooted entirely in Yehezkel's throne-chariot vision and the ancient Afro-Asiatic covenant priestly tradition. The divine beings, angelic priests, and chariot throne described here belong to the authentic pre-Christian covenant cosmological framework. The minimal concern noted is entirely external: certain early post-Christian mystical writers who were aware of Jewish liturgical traditions later referenced similar angelic worship themes. This borrowing occurred after the fact and had nothing to do with the original text or the Qumran covenant community's theology. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice was written independently of and before any Christian tradition. Read this text as what it is: authentic Qumran covenant liturgy celebrating the heavenly worship of Creator — with zero connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the Maskil. Song of the burnt offering of the thirteenth Sabbath on the ninety-first day.

2 Praise the Creator of all divine beings O you who are exalted above all in the inner chambers of his glory.

3 How wonderful is the appearance of the throne of his glory and how marvellous are the works of his light.

4 The living creatures of the holy of holies appear before his glory — the appearance of streams of fire is between them, in the appearance of their wondrous brilliance.

5 And the robe of the inner chambers of the God of gods is like the appearance of sapphire, of beryl and chrysolite — the radiance of the spirit of the living creatures.

6 Holy spirits — all their decorations are like the weaving of wondrous works woven together — the spirits of living creatures move and return like the appearance of lightning.

7 The chariot throne of his glory sings with the voice of divine stillness — the holy ones attend upon him — they exult in his wonderful praises.

8 And between the holy wheels of glory is the splendour of the chariot throne — and beneath his glory the clouds of fire burst forth.

9 The firmament of light is the robe of his royal glory — the appearance of glowing amber surrounds the Glorious One.

10 And from beneath the throne of his glory come the sounds of blessing — every divine being blesses him in the dwelling of his glory.

11 Voices of divine blessing fill all the foundations of the inner sanctuary — the sound of their blessing is like the roar of many waters.

12 Blessed be the Creator of all divine beings forever and ever from the firmament of his glory — his praise is above all the holy ones and above all the divine beings forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q405 and MasShirShabb preserve Song 13, the culminating song of the thirteen-song cycle. Song 13 describes the full Merkabah vision — the throne, the robe of glory, the living creatures, the fire, the firmament, and the divine blessing — in the most elaborate and detailed language of the entire collection. The ninety-first day on which Song 13 is sung is the last day of the first quarter of the 364-day year — the cycle of thirteen songs completes the first quarter of the covenant year, and the climactic chariot-throne vision marks the completion of that quarter.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves both Yehezkel's chariot visions and the throne vision of Chanokh Chapter 14 in full — both source texts for Song 13's climactic description. The appearance of sapphire and beryl and chrysolite — verse 5 — draws on Yehezkel 1:26 and 10:1. The glowing amber that surrounds the Glorious One — verse 9 — draws on Yehezkel 1:27. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice weaves together both covenant visionary traditions — Yehezkelian and Enochic — into a single liturgical text of extraordinary richness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yehezkel 1:26-28 — and above the firmament over their heads there was the likeness of a throne, in appearance like sapphire — and seated above the throne was a likeness with a human appearance — and downward from what had the appearance of his waist I saw as it were gleaming metal, like the appearance of fire enclosed all around — provides the primary source for Song 13's final throne description. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice transforms this covenant prophetic description into participatory liturgy: what Yehezkel saw the community sings.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Song 13 is the theological summit of the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice and one of the most remarkable liturgical texts in the ancient covenant world. The thirteen-song cycle moves systematically from the establishment of the seven angelic priesthoods in Song 1 through the progressive revelation of the heavenly sanctuary to the full vision of the chariot throne in Songs 12-13. The entire cycle is a liturgical journey — the earthly community participates week by week in an ascent through the heavenly sanctuary until it reaches the throne of Creator's glory. This participatory ascent theology is the Songs' most distinctive and profound theological contribution.

SONGS OF THE SABBATH SACRIFICE -- COMPLETE

Volume Forty-Two -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

BLESSED BE THE CREATOR OF ALL DIVINE BEINGS FOREVER

HIS PRAISE IS ABOVE ALL THE HOLY ONES AND ABOVE ALL THE DIVINE BEINGS

Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice Song 13 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q405
